title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, caste, color, religion, sexual identity orientation. pledge act interact ways contribute open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, healthy community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment community include: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes, learning experience Focusing best just us individuals, overall community Examples unacceptable behavior include: use sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information, physical email address, without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders responsible clarifying enforcing standards acceptable behavior take appropriate fair corrective action response behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful. Community leaders right responsibility remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, communicate reasons moderation decisions appropriate.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct applies within community spaces, also applies individual officially representing community public spaces. Examples representing community include using official e-mail address, posting via official social media account, acting appointed representative online offline event.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Instances abusive, harassing, otherwise unacceptable behavior may reported community leaders responsible enforcement anonymous@example.com. complaints reviewed investigated promptly fairly. community leaders obligated respect privacy security reporter incident.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders follow Community Impact Guidelines determining consequences action deem violation Code Conduct:

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Use inappropriate language behavior deemed unprofessional unwelcome community. Consequence: private, written warning community leaders, providing clarity around nature violation explanation behavior inappropriate. public apology may requested.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: violation single incident series actions. Consequence: warning consequences continued behavior. interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, specified period time. includes avoiding interactions community spaces well external channels like social media. Violating terms may lead temporary permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: serious violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior. Consequence: temporary ban sort interaction public communication community specified period time. public private interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, allowed period. Violating terms may lead permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: sspLNIRT package provides sample size planning tools item calibration Joint Hierarchical Model (JHM; van der Linden, 2007). JHM combines two-parameter normal ogive model response accuracy log-normal model response times, estimated via Gibbs sampling LNIRT package (Fox et al., 2023). central question package addresses : Given assumptions data-generating process, minimum number respondents needed achieve desired level item parameter accuracy? Accuracy defined root mean squared error (RMSE) target item parameter falling user-specified threshold. package supports three workflows, described separate section vignette: Shiny app — interactive interface exploring precomputed results generating customized R script. R package precomputed results — programmatic access database 2,400 precomputed design conditions, functions retrieval, inspection, visualization. Custom simulations — running comp_rmse() optim_sample() design conditions covered precomputed grid.
code: # Install from GitHub if (!requireNamespace("devtools")) install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("anonymous-peer-2026/sspLNIRT") library(sspLNIRT)

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: package includes interactive Shiny application provides accessible entry point. Launch : app allows users : Select design parameters (test length, mean item discrimination, residual variance level, ability–speed correlation) target RMSE thresholds via dropdown menus. Retrieve corresponding minimum sample size precomputed database, together estimation metrics (RMSE, Monte Carlo SD, bias) item person parameters. Inspect diagnostic plots including estimation accuracy across parameter values, power curves, convergence diagnostics, simulated response accuracy response time distributions. Download result objects (.rds) use R. design conditions covered precomputed grid, app can generate customized R script appropriate function calls, can executed locally computing cluster. Detailed documentation app interface available https://github.com/anonymous-peer-2026/sspLNIRT/.
code: run_app()

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: section walks full workflow retrieving inspecting precomputed results programmatically. precomputed database covers 2,400 design conditions; specification falls within grid, simulations needed.

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: Start inspecting design conditions available precomputed grid using available_configs(): row represents one precomputed optimization run. columns correspond design factors systematically varied: thresh (RMSE threshold), .par (target item parameter), K (test length), mu.alpha (mean item discrimination), meanlog.sigma2 (log-scale mean residual variance σ2\sigma^2), rho (ability–speed correlation). model parameters (e.g., mean item difficulty, item parameter correlations, standard deviations) held constant across grid. values stored design element result (see Section 2.3). check available levels per factor:
code: configs <- available_configs() head(configs, 10) #>    thresh out.par  K mu.alpha meanlog.sigma2 rho #> 1    0.20     phi 50      1.4         log(1) 0.4 #> 2    0.10   alpha 50      1.0         log(1) 0.6 #> 3    0.10    beta 30      0.8       log(0.2) 0.6 #> 4    0.15   alpha 50      1.4       log(0.2) 0.2 #> 5    0.15    beta 50      0.6         log(1) 0.2 #> 6    0.05    beta 50      1.0       log(0.2) 0.2 #> 7    0.15   alpha 50      1.2       log(0.4) 0.2 #> 8    0.10  lambda 50      1.0       log(0.4) 0.6 #> 9    0.10     phi 50      0.8       log(0.6) 0.6 #> 10   0.05   alpha 50      1.4       log(0.4) 0.6 unique(configs$out.par) #> [1] "phi"    "alpha"  "beta"   "lambda" unique(configs$thresh) #> [1] 0.20 0.10 0.15 0.05 unique(configs$K) #> [1] 50 30 unique(configs$mu.alpha) #> [1] 1.4 1.0 0.8 0.6 1.2 unique(configs$meanlog.sigma2) #> [1] "log(1)"   "log(0.2)" "log(0.4)" "log(0.6)" "log(0.8)" unique(configs$rho) #> [1] 0.4 0.6 0.2

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: get_sspLNIRT() function looks precomputed results matching six design factors. accepts vectors thresh .par target multiple item parameters simultaneously: precomputed database stores results target parameter separately, get_sspLNIRT() matches .par/thresh pair independently. returns bottleneck result — parameter required largest sample size, since NN guarantees parameters also meet (less demanding) thresholds. summary() method provides concise overview: planning based single parameter, scalar inputs work way:

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: object returned get_sspLNIRT() list two elements: N.min — minimum sample size identified bisection optimizer. lower bound already satisfied threshold, string "res.lb < thresh"; upper bound insufficient, "res.ub >   thresh". res.best — RMSE target parameter N.min. comp.rmse — full accuracy evaluation N.min, including RMSE, MC SD, bias item person parameters, well binned error data convergence diagnostics. trace — optimization trace: sample sizes RMSE values bisection step, number steps, elapsed time. result$design: full set input parameters used precomputation (class sspLNIRT.design). includes constant parameters varied across grid: Notably, result$design$.par result$design$thresh identify single-parameter configuration bottleneck: tells parameter drove minimum sample size. multi-parameter call , returned N.min diagnostics correspond optimization run bottleneck parameter.
code: str(result$design, max.level = 1) #> List of 19 #>  $ thresh        : num 0.1 #>  $ range         : num [1:2] 50 2000 #>  $ out.par       : chr "alpha" #>  $ iter          : num 200 #>  $ K             : num 30 #>  $ mu.person     : num [1:2] 0 0 #>  $ mu.item       : num [1:4] 1 0 0.5 1 #>  $ meanlog.sigma2: num -0.511 #>  $ cov.m.person  : num [1:2, 1:2] 1 0.4 0.4 1 #>  $ cov.m.item    : num [1:4, 1:4] 1 0 0 0 0 1 0 0.4 0 0 ... #>  $ sd.item       : num [1:4] 0.2 1 0.2 0.5 #>  $ cor2cov.item  : logi TRUE #>  $ sdlog.sigma2  : num 0 #>  $ item.pars.m   : NULL #>  $ XG            : num 5000 #>  $ burnin        : num 20 #>  $ seed          : num 311971 #>  $ keep.rhat.dat : logi TRUE #>  $ keep.err.dat  : logi FALSE #>  - attr(*, "class")= chr "sspLNIRT.design" result$design$out.par #> [1] "alpha" result$design$thresh #> [1] 0.1

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: design object hand, can inspect assumed parameter values imply observable data. plot_RA() plot_RT() functions simulate data specified model display resulting distributions. Using result$design avoids re-specify constant parameters manually: Item-level response accuracy. .theta = TRUE, plot_RA() shows item characteristic curves (ICCs) — probability correct response function ability θ\theta. panel corresponds one item. dashed vertical line marks item difficulty (β\beta), faint dots show simulated binary responses:  Setting .theta = FALSE shows marginal distribution response probabilities across persons item:  Person-level response accuracy. total correct score distribution provides summary expected test performance:  .theta = TRUE variant shows mean total score function ability:  Item-level response times. panel shows RT density one item. logRT argument toggles seconds log scale:   Person-level response times. Quantile reference lines summarize distribution mean response times across persons:   plots help evaluate whether assumed data-generating process produces plausible score time distributions intended application. may also inform adjustments input parameters committing sample size.
code: cfg <- result$design sim.mod.args <- list(   K              = cfg$K,   mu.person      = cfg$mu.person,   mu.item        = cfg$mu.item,   meanlog.sigma2 = cfg$meanlog.sigma2,   sdlog.sigma2   = cfg$sdlog.sigma2,   cov.m.person   = cfg$cov.m.person,   cov.m.item     = cfg$cov.m.item,   sd.item        = cfg$sd.item,   cor2cov.item   = cfg$cor2cov.item ) do.call(plot_RA, c(list(level = "item", by.theta = TRUE, N = 1e4), sim.mod.args)) do.call(plot_RA, c(list(level = "item", by.theta = FALSE, N = 1e4), sim.mod.args)) do.call(plot_RA, c(list(level = "person", by.theta = FALSE, N = 1e4), sim.mod.args)) do.call(plot_RA, c(list(level = "person", by.theta = TRUE, N = 1e4), sim.mod.args)) do.call(plot_RT, c(list(level = "item", logRT = FALSE, N = 1e4), sim.mod.args)) do.call(plot_RT, c(list(level = "item", logRT = TRUE, N = 1e4), sim.mod.args)) do.call(plot_RT, c(list(level = "person", logRT = TRUE, N = 1e4), sim.mod.args)) do.call(plot_RT, c(list(level = "person", logRT = FALSE, N = 1e4), sim.mod.args))

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: plot_estimation() function displays RMSE bias function true (simulated) parameter values minimum NN. can quantify extent estimation accuracy varies systematically across parameter range. instance, much items extreme difficulty values tend estimated less precisely. item parameters:   person parameters:  point represents mean value within quantile bin true parameter. smooth line LOESS fit.
code: plot_estimation(result$object, pars = "item", y.val = "rmse") plot_estimation(result$object, pars = "item", y.val = "bias") plot_estimation(result$object, pars = "person", y.val = "rmse")

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: plot_power_curve() function fits log-log regression bisection trace displays relationship sample size RMSE:  dashed horizontal line marks RMSE threshold; dotted vertical line marks minimum NN. left panel shows log-log fit; right panel shows relationship original scale. R2R^2 log-log regression reported caption.
code: plot_power_curve(result$object, out.par = result$design$out.par,                  thresh = result$design$thresh)

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: Convergence MCMC chains can assessed R̂\hat{R} diagnostics stored result object. quantiles R̂\hat{R} statistic (Vehtari et al., 2021) across Monte Carlo iterations: Values 1 indicate chains mixed well. rows represent quantiles (50th, 80th, 90th, 95th percentile); columns correspond item parameters.

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: intended design covered precomputed grid, package provides two functions running simulations directly: comp_rmse() evaluates item parameter accuracy single, fixed sample size. optim_sample() runs bisection optimizer find minimum sample size target RMSE threshold. functions computationally intensive (minutes hours depending settings) benefit parallel execution.

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: simulation estimation parallelized Monte Carlo replications using future framework. Set parallel backend calling either function:
code: library(future) plan(multisession, workers = 4)

what: 3.2 Evaluate accuracy at a fixed N
title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: comp_rmse() simulates data, fits model, computes RMSE, MC SD, bias item person parameters specified sample size: Key arguments: iter: number Monte Carlo replications (simulated datasets). Higher values reduce MC standard deviation RMSE estimate. XG: number Gibbs sampler iterations per chain (4 chains run per replication). keep.err.dat: TRUE, retains full per-replication error data (needed plot_estimation() custom binning). FALSE, errors pre-binned K quantile bins. keep.rhat.dat: TRUE, retains R̂\hat{R} matrix across replications. seed: passed future.apply parallel-safe reproducibility. output sspLNIRT.object class optim_sample() results works summary(), plot_estimation(), print().
code: rmse_result <- comp_rmse(   N              = 200,   iter           = 200,   K              = 30,   mu.person      = c(0, 0),   mu.item        = c(1, 0, 0.5, 1),   meanlog.sigma2 = log(0.6),   cov.m.person   = matrix(c(1, 0.4, 0.4, 1), ncol = 2),   cov.m.item     = matrix(c(1, 0, 0, 0,                              0, 1, 0, 0.4,                              0, 0, 1, 0,                              0, 0.4, 0, 1), ncol = 4),   sd.item        = c(0.2, 1, 0.2, 0.5),   cor2cov.item   = TRUE,   sdlog.sigma2   = 0,   XG             = 5000,   burnin         = 20,   seed           = 1234,   keep.err.dat   = TRUE,   keep.rhat.dat  = TRUE )  summary(rmse_result)

what: 3.3 Optimize for the minimum sample size
title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: optim_sample() wraps comp_rmse() bisection loop. accepts vectors thresh .par simultaneously target multiple item parameters: range argument sets search interval NN. bisection halves interval step, evaluating comp_rmse() midpoint, refinement possible (.e., interval width one). number bisection steps approximately ⌈log2(range[2]−range[1])⌉\lceil \log_2(\text{range}[2] - \text{range}[1]) \rceil, requiring full comp_rmse() evaluation. multiple parameters targeted, bisection requires specified parameters simultaneously fall respective thresholds narrowing search interval, resulting NN satisfies targets . differs get_sspLNIRT() approach, looks parameter independently selects bottleneck post hoc. result object supports inspection functions demonstrated Section 2:
code: custom_result <- optim_sample(   thresh         = c(0.10, 0.15),   out.par        = c("alpha", "beta"),   range          = c(50, 2000),   iter           = 200,   K              = 30,   mu.person      = c(0, 0),   mu.item        = c(1, 0, 0.5, 1),   meanlog.sigma2 = log(0.6),   cov.m.person   = matrix(c(1, 0.4, 0.4, 1), ncol = 2),   cov.m.item     = matrix(c(1, 0, 0, 0,                              0, 1, 0, 0.4,                              0, 0, 1, 0,                              0, 0.4, 0, 1), ncol = 4),   sd.item        = c(0.2, 1, 0.2, 0.5),   cor2cov.item   = TRUE,   sdlog.sigma2   = 0,   XG             = 5000,   burnin         = 20,   seed           = 1234,   keep.err.dat   = FALSE,   keep.rhat.dat  = TRUE )  summary(custom_result) plot_estimation(custom_result, pars = "item", y.val = "rmse") plot_power_curve(custom_result, out.par = "alpha", thresh = 0.10)

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT
text: Computation time: single comp_rmse() call iter = 200 XG = 5000 can take minutes hours depending NN, KK, available cores. full bisection typically requires 10–13 evaluations, optim_sample() can take several hours. Parallelization: setting future::plan(multisession) multiple workers distributes replications across cores. number workers match available cores. Reproducibility: seed argument ensures parallel-safe reproducibility via L’Ecuyer-CMRG streams. Pass explicit integer full reproducibility. Memory: keep.err.dat = TRUE, full error arrays retained memory. large NN KK, setting FALSE reduces memory usage pre-binning errors.
code: # Reset to sequential when done plan(sequential)

title: Sample Size Planning with sspLNIRT

text: sspLNIRT sample size planning tool item calibration Joint Hierarchical Model (JHM) response accuracy response time. estimates minimum sample size required achieve target accuracy (RMSE) item parameter estimates specified data-generating process. package provides: Precomputed results various design conditions, accessible instantly via Shiny app /R package. Custom Sample Size Estimation via optim_sample() design conditions outside precomputed data. Diagnostic functions inspecting parameter accuracy bias, power curves, implied response time response accuracy distributions.

title: List Available Precomputed Configurations — available_configs
text: Returns data frame summarizing parameter combinations precomputed [optim_sample()] results available [sspLNIRT.data]. Use check values `thresh`, `.par`, `K`, `mu.alpha`, `meanlog.sigma2`, `rho` can passed [get_sspLNIRT()].

title: List Available Precomputed Configurations — available_configs
code: available_configs()

title: List Available Precomputed Configurations — available_configs
text: data frame one row per precomputed configuration   following columns: `thresh` RMSE threshold. `.par` Target item parameter. `K` Test length. `mu.alpha` Mean \(\alpha\) (item discrimination). `meanlog.sigma2` Mean \(\log(\sigma^2)\). `rho` Correlation \(\theta\) \(\zeta\).

title: List Available Precomputed Configurations — available_configs
code: configs <- available_configs() head(configs) #>   thresh out.par  K mu.alpha meanlog.sigma2 rho #> 1   0.20     phi 50      1.4         log(1) 0.4 #> 2   0.10   alpha 50      1.0         log(1) 0.6 #> 3   0.10    beta 30      0.8       log(0.2) 0.6 #> 4   0.15   alpha 50      1.4       log(0.2) 0.2 #> 5   0.15    beta 50      0.6         log(1) 0.2 #> 6   0.05    beta 50      1.0       log(0.2) 0.2  # unique test lengths unique(configs$K) #> [1] 50 30

what: Compute RMSE of Parameter Estimates using Monte-Carlo Simulations — comp_rmse
title: Compute RMSE of Parameter Estimates using Monte-Carlo Simulations — comp_rmse
text: Computes root mean squared error (RMSE), Monte Carlo standard deviation, bias estimated item person parameters based simulated data. Data generated Joint Hierarchical Model (JHM) using two-parameter normal ogive model response accuracy log-normal model response times. Estimation performed via MCMC (Gibbs sampling) four parallel chains per replication using [LNIRT::LNIRT()].

title: Compute RMSE of Parameter Estimates using Monte-Carlo Simulations — comp_rmse
code: comp_rmse(   N,   iter = 200,   K = 30,   mu.person = c(0, 0),   mu.item = c(1, 0, 0.5, 1),   meanlog.sigma2 = log(0.6),   cov.m.person = matrix(c(1, 0.4, 0.4, 1), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE),   cov.m.item = matrix(c(0.2, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0.2, 0, 0, 0.2, 0, 0, 0.2, 0, 0.5), ncol =     4, byrow = TRUE),   sdlog.sigma2 = 0,   item.pars.m = NULL,   cor2cov.item = FALSE,   sd.item = NULL,   XG = 5000,   burnin = 20,   seed = NULL,   keep.err.dat = FALSE,   keep.rhat.dat = FALSE )

title: Compute RMSE of Parameter Estimates using Monte-Carlo Simulations — comp_rmse
text: N Integer. sample size (number persons). Must >= 2. iter Integer. number Monte Carlo replications (simulated data sets). Default 200. K Integer. test length (number items). Default 30. mu.person Numeric vector length 2. Population means person parameters \((\theta, \zeta)\). mu.item Numeric vector length 4. Population means item parameters \((\alpha, \beta, \varphi, \lambda)\). meanlog.sigma2 Numeric. Mean log-normal distribution residual variance \(\sigma^2\) response time model. cov.m.person 2x2 symmetric matrix. Covariance (correlation) matrix \((\theta, \zeta)\). cov.m.item 4x4 symmetric matrix. Covariance (correlation) matrix \((\alpha, \beta, \varphi, \lambda)\). See `cor2cov.item`. sdlog.sigma2 Numeric. Standard deviation log-normal distribution \(\sigma^2\). Default 0 (constant across items). item.pars.m Matrix 4 columns `NULL`. supplied, item parameters held constant across replications (rows = items, columns = \(\alpha, \beta, \varphi, \lambda\)). cor2cov.item Logical. `TRUE`, `cov.m.item` treated correlation matrix converted covariance matrix using `sd.item`. sd.item Numeric vector length 4 `NULL`. Standard deviations \((\alpha, \beta, \varphi, \lambda)\). Required `cor2cov.item = TRUE`. XG Integer. Number Gibbs sampler iterations per chain. Default 5000. burnin Integer. Burn-percentage (0–99). Default 20. seed Integer `TRUE`. Random seed passed [future.apply::future_lapply()] via `future.seed`. integer gives reproducible L'Ecuyer-CMRG seed sequence; `TRUE` (default `NULL` supplied) generates random parallel-safe seed. See **Note**. keep.err.dat Logical. `TRUE`, full per-replication error data returned. `FALSE` (default), errors binned `K` quantile bins per parameter. keep.rhat.dat Logical. `TRUE`, full \(\hat{R}\) matrix (items x replications) returned. `FALSE` (default), discarded.

title: Compute RMSE of Parameter Estimates using Monte-Carlo Simulations — comp_rmse
text: list S3 class `"sspLNIRT"` containing: `person` List named vectors `rmse`, `mc.sd.rmse`, `bias`     \(\theta\) \(\zeta\). `item` List named vectors `rmse`, `mc.sd.rmse`, `bias`     \(\alpha\), \(\beta\), \(\varphi\), \(\lambda\),     \(\sigma^2\). `rhat.dat` Matrix (items x replications) \(\hat{R}\) values     `keep.rhat.dat = TRUE`, otherwise `NULL`. `err.dat` List data frames `person` `item`.     `keep.err.dat = FALSE`, errors binned (columns: `par`, `bin`,     `mean_sim`, `mean_err`, `mean_rmse`). `TRUE`, raw per-replication     errors (columns: `rep`, `par`, `sim.val`, `err`).

title: Compute RMSE of Parameter Estimates using Monte-Carlo Simulations — comp_rmse
text: `seed = NULL` passed, internally converted `TRUE`, triggers automatic parallel-safe seeding via [future.apply::future_lapply()]. Pass explicit integer full reproducibility. Computation parallelized replications using future/future.apply framework. Set parallel backend (e.g., `future::plan(future::multisession)`) calling function.

title: Compute RMSE of Parameter Estimates using Monte-Carlo Simulations — comp_rmse
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Minimal example future::plan(future::multisession, workers = 2)  result <- comp_rmse(   N              = 100,   iter           = 3,   K              = 10,   mu.person      = c(0, 0),   mu.item        = c(1, 0, 0.5, 1),   meanlog.sigma2 = log(0.6),   cov.m.person   = matrix(c(1, 0.4, 0.4, 1), ncol = 2),   cov.m.item     = diag(4),   sd.item        = c(0.2, 1, 0.2, 0.5),   cor2cov.item   = TRUE,   sdlog.sigma2   = 0,   XG             = 500,   burnin         = 20,   seed           = 42,   keep.err.dat   = FALSE,   keep.rhat.dat  = TRUE )  result$item$rmse result$person$rmse  future::plan(future::sequential) } # }

what: Retrieve Precomputed Sample Size Results — get_sspLNIRT
title: Retrieve Precomputed Sample Size Results — get_sspLNIRT
text: Looks precomputed [optim_sample()] results internal dataset [sspLNIRT.data]. target parameter / threshold pair, function finds matching configuration returns result whose minimum \(N\) bottleneck (.e., largest required sample size across requested parameters). multiple target parameters supplied, matched independently precomputed grid. returned result corresponds whichever single-parameter configuration demanded largest \(N\), since \(N\) guarantees parameters also meet thresholds (RMSE monotonically decreasing \(N\)).

title: Retrieve Precomputed Sample Size Results — get_sspLNIRT
code: get_sspLNIRT(thresh, out.par, K, mu.alpha, meanlog.sigma2, rho)

title: Retrieve Precomputed Sample Size Results — get_sspLNIRT
text: thresh Numeric vector. Target RMSE threshold(s). length `.par`. .par Character vector. Item parameter(s): one `"alpha"`, `"beta"`, `"phi"`, `"lambda"`. K Integer. Test length. mu.alpha Numeric. Population mean discrimination parameter. meanlog.sigma2 Numeric. Mean log-normal distribution \(\sigma^2\) (log scale). rho Numeric \([-1, 1]\). Correlation \(\theta\) \(\zeta\).

title: Retrieve Precomputed Sample Size Results — get_sspLNIRT
text: list components: `object` object class `"sspLNIRT"` returned     [optim_sample()]. `design` list full set parameter values used     precomputation.

title: Retrieve Precomputed Sample Size Results — get_sspLNIRT
text: Searches [sspLNIRT.data] row row using approximate matching (`tol = 1e-3`) numeric parameters. exact match found, error message reports parameters differed closest available configuration. ## Bottleneck selection matching `.par[j]` / `thresh[j]` pair independently, bottleneck result selected : 1. `N.min == "res.ub > thresh"`, result returned. 2. Otherwise, result largest numeric `N.min` returned. 3. Otherwise (`N.min == "res.lb < thresh"`), first result    returned.

title: Retrieve Precomputed Sample Size Results — get_sspLNIRT
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ result <- get_sspLNIRT(   thresh         = 0.10,   out.par        = "alpha",   K              = 30,   mu.alpha       = 1,   meanlog.sigma2 = log(0.6),   rho            = 0.2 ) summary(result$object) } # }

what: Optimize the Minimum Sample Size using Monte Carlo Simulations — optim_sample
title: Optimize the Minimum Sample Size using Monte Carlo Simulations — optim_sample
text: Finds minimum sample size \(N\) root mean squared error (RMSE) one target item parameters falls specified threshold. search uses bisection algorithm: step, simulated data generated estimated via [comp_rmse()], search interval halved depending whether RMSE target met. Data simulated Joint Hierarchical Model (JHM) using two-parameter normal ogive model response accuracy log-normal model response times.

title: Optimize the Minimum Sample Size using Monte Carlo Simulations — optim_sample
code: optim_sample(   out.par = "alpha",   thresh,   range = c(50, 2000),   iter = 200,   K = 30,   mu.person = c(0, 0),   mu.item = c(1, 0, 0.5, 1),   meanlog.sigma2 = log(0.6),   cov.m.person = matrix(c(1, 0.4, 0.4, 1), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE),   cov.m.item = matrix(c(0.2, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0.2, 0, 0, 0.2, 0, 0, 0.2, 0, 0.5), ncol =     4, byrow = TRUE),   sdlog.sigma2 = 0,   item.pars.m = NULL,   cor2cov.item = FALSE,   sd.item = NULL,   seed = NULL,   XG = 5000,   burnin = 20,   keep.err.dat = FALSE,   keep.rhat.dat = FALSE,   verbose = interactive() )

title: Optimize the Minimum Sample Size using Monte Carlo Simulations — optim_sample
text: .par Character vector. Name(s) target item parameter(s), one `"alpha"`, `"beta"`, `"phi"`, `"lambda"`. Order must match `thresh`. thresh Numeric vector. Target RMSE threshold(s) item parameter(s) named `.par`. Must positive length `.par`. range Integer vector length 2. Lower upper bounds sample size search interval. Must satisfy `range[1] < range[2]` `range[1] >= 2`. iter Integer. Number Monte Carlo replications per \(N\) evaluation. Default 200. K Integer. Test length (number items). Default 30. mu.person Numeric vector length 2. Population means \((\theta, \zeta)\). mu.item Numeric vector length 4. Population means \((\alpha, \beta, \varphi, \lambda)\). meanlog.sigma2 Numeric. Mean log-normal distribution residual variance \(\sigma^2\). cov.m.person 2x2 symmetric matrix. Covariance matrix \((\theta, \zeta)\). cov.m.item 4x4 symmetric matrix. Covariance (correlation) matrix \((\alpha, \beta, \varphi, \lambda)\). See `cor2cov.item`. sdlog.sigma2 Numeric. Standard deviation log-normal distribution \(\sigma^2\). Default 0. item.pars.m Matrix 4 columns `NULL`. supplied, item parameters held constant across replications. cor2cov.item Logical. `TRUE`, `cov.m.item` treated correlation matrix converted using `sd.item`. sd.item Numeric vector length 4 `NULL`. Standard deviations item parameters. Required `cor2cov.item = TRUE`. seed Integer, `TRUE`, `NULL`. Passed [comp_rmse()] parallel-safe seeding. XG Integer. Number Gibbs sampler iterations per chain. Default 5000. burnin Integer. Burn-percentage (0–99). Default 20. keep.err.dat Logical. Whether retain full error data [comp_rmse()] output optimal \(N\). keep.rhat.dat Logical. Whether retain full \(\hat{R}\) matrix [comp_rmse()] output optimal \(N\). verbose Logical. `TRUE`, progress information emitted via [message()] (can suppressed [suppressMessages()]). Defaults [interactive()].

title: Optimize the Minimum Sample Size using Monte Carlo Simulations — optim_sample
text: list S3 class `"sspLNIRT"` containing: `N.min` Integer: minimum sample size met thresholds.     lower bound already satisfies threshold, character     string `"res.lb < thresh"` returned. upper bound     satisfy threshold, `"res.ub > thresh"` returned. `res.best` Named numeric vector. RMSE target parameter(s)     optimal \(N\) (boundary triggered early     stopping). `comp.rmse` List. Full [comp_rmse()] output optimal     \(N\). `trace` List optimization diagnostics (`steps`,     `track.res`, `track.N`, `time.taken`).

title: Optimize the Minimum Sample Size using Monte Carlo Simulations — optim_sample
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ future::plan(future::multisession, workers = 2)  result <- optim_sample(   thresh         = c(0.10, 0.15),   out.par        = c("alpha", "beta"),   range          = c(100, 500),   iter           = 5,   K              = 10,   mu.person      = c(0, 0),   mu.item        = c(1, 0, 0.5, 1),   meanlog.sigma2 = log(0.3),   cov.m.person   = matrix(c(1, 0.5, 0.5, 1), ncol = 2),   cov.m.item     = matrix(c(1, 0,   0,   0,                              0, 1,   0,   0.3,                              0, 0,   1,   0,                              0, 0.3, 0,   1), ncol = 4),   sd.item        = c(0.2, 0.5, 0.2, 0.5),   cor2cov.item   = TRUE,   sdlog.sigma2   = 0.2,   XG             = 500,   seed           = 42 )  summary(result)  future::plan(future::sequential) } # }

what: Plot Method for sspLNIRT Objects — plot.sspLNIRT
title: Plot Method for sspLNIRT Objects — plot.sspLNIRT
text: S3 method [base::plot()] generic, dispatched objects class `"sspLNIRT"` (returned [optim_sample()], [comp_rmse()], [get_sspLNIRT()]). `type` argument selects visualization: `"estimation"` (default) Per-parameter RMSE bias function     true (simulated) parameter value, faceted parameter. See     [plot_estimation()]. `"power_curve"` Optimization trace log-log power curve plus     original-scale view, RMSE threshold minimum \(N\)     overlaid. Requires [optim_sample()] result. See     [plot_power_curve()]. Arguments specific `type` passed `...`.

title: Plot Method for sspLNIRT Objects — plot.sspLNIRT
code: # S3 method for class 'sspLNIRT' plot(x, y = NULL, type = c("estimation", "power_curve"), ...)

title: Plot Method for sspLNIRT Objects — plot.sspLNIRT
text: x object class `"sspLNIRT"`. y Unused (required generic). Ignored warning supplied. type Character. One `"estimation"` `"power_curve"`. ... Additional arguments passed underlying plot helper. `type = "estimation"`: `pars` (`"item"` / `"person"`), `y.val` (`"rmse"` / `"bias"`), `n.bins`. `type = "power_curve"`: `.par`, `thresh`.

title: Plot Method for sspLNIRT Objects — plot.sspLNIRT
text: [ggplot2::ggplot] object.

title: Plot Method for sspLNIRT Objects — plot.sspLNIRT
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ result <- get_sspLNIRT(   thresh = 0.10, out.par = "alpha",   K = 30, mu.alpha = 1,   meanlog.sigma2 = log(0.6), rho = 0.2 )  # estimation accuracy plot(result$object, type = "estimation", pars = "item", y.val = "rmse")  # power curve from the optimization trace plot(result$object, type = "power_curve",      out.par = "alpha", thresh = 0.10) } # }

title: Plot Simulated Response Accuracy — plot_RA
text: Simulates data Joint Hierarchical Model plots resulting response accuracy. plot type depends `level`, `.theta` `overlay`: - **Person, distribution**: Histogram total correct scores across   persons. - **Person, theta**: Mean total score function ability   \(\theta\), shown bar chart binned \(\theta\). - **Item, distribution**: Density \(P(X = 1)\) across persons   item, item-wise median marked. - **Item, theta**: Item characteristic curves (ICCs), showing   \(P(X = 1)\) function \(\theta\) item,   dashed line item difficulty \(\beta\). - **Item, `overlay = TRUE`**: item curves drawn single panel   instead facet grid. Data generated via [sim.jhm.data()] `scale = FALSE`.

title: Plot Simulated Response Accuracy — plot_RA
code: plot_RA(   design = NULL,   level = "item",   by.theta = TRUE,   overlay = FALSE,   N = 1000,   K = 30,   mu.person = c(0, 0),   mu.item = c(1, 0, 0.5, 1),   meanlog.sigma2 = log(0.6),   cov.m.person = matrix(c(1, 0.4, 0.4, 1), ncol = 2, byrow = FALSE),   cov.m.item = matrix(c(1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0.4, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0.4, 0, 1), ncol = 4,     byrow = TRUE),   sd.item = c(0.2, 1, 0.2, 0.5),   sdlog.sigma2 = 0,   item.pars.m = NULL,   cor2cov.item = FALSE )

title: Plot Simulated Response Accuracy — plot_RA
text: design Optional list `"sspLNIRT.design"` object holding data-generating design. supplied, fields used design-related argument caller pass explicitly (`K`, `mu.person`, `mu.item`, `meanlog.sigma2`, `cov.m.person`, `cov.m.item`, `sd.item`, `sdlog.sigma2`, `item.pars.m`, `cor2cov.item`). Caller- supplied arguments always take precedence. Extra fields `design` (e.g. `thresh`, `.par`, `seed`) ignored, object returned `$design` [get_sspLNIRT()] [optim_sample()] can passed directly. Default `NULL` (use function defaults / caller arguments). level Character. `"person"` person-level aggregates `"item"` item-level curves / distributions. .theta Logical. `TRUE`, x-axis \(\theta\); `FALSE`, marginal distribution shown. overlay Logical. `TRUE` `level = "item"`, item curves drawn one panel rather faceted. N Integer. Sample size (number persons) simulated data. Default 1000. K Integer. Test length (number items). Default 10. mu.person Numeric vector length 2. Population means \((\theta, \zeta)\). mu.item Numeric vector length 4. Population means \((\alpha, \beta, \varphi, \lambda)\). meanlog.sigma2 Numeric. Mean log-normal distribution \(\sigma^2\) (log scale). cov.m.person 2x2 symmetric matrix. Covariance matrix \((\theta, \zeta)\). cov.m.item 4x4 symmetric matrix. Covariance (correlation) matrix \((\alpha, \beta, \varphi, \lambda)\). See `cor2cov.item`. sd.item Numeric vector length 4. Standard deviations \((\alpha, \beta, \varphi, \lambda)\). Required `cor2cov.item = TRUE`. sdlog.sigma2 Numeric. Standard deviation log-normal distribution \(\sigma^2\). Default 0. item.pars.m Matrix 4 columns `NULL`. supplied, item parameters held fixed instead drawn truncated MVN. cor2cov.item Logical. `TRUE`, `cov.m.item` treated correlation matrix converted using `sd.item`.

title: Plot Simulated Response Accuracy — plot_RA
text: [ggplot2::ggplot] object.

title: Plot Simulated Response Accuracy — plot_RA
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ plot_RA(level = "person", by.theta = TRUE, N = 500, K = 20) plot_RA(level = "item", by.theta = FALSE, N = 1000, K = 5,         mu.item = c(1, 0, 0.5, 1), sd.item = c(0.2, 0.5, 0.2, 0.5))  # Many items: overlay instead of faceting plot_RA(level = "item", by.theta = TRUE, overlay = TRUE, N = 1000, K = 30)  # Pass a design object retrieved from get_sspLNIRT() (or optim_sample()): res <- get_sspLNIRT(thresh = 0.10, out.par = "alpha",                     K = 30, mu.alpha = 1,                     meanlog.sigma2 = log(0.6), rho = 0.4) plot_RA(res$design, level = "item", by.theta = TRUE) } # }

title: Plot Simulated Response Times — plot_RT
text: Simulates data Joint Hierarchical Model plots resulting response time distribution. plot type depends `level`, `logRT` `overlay`: - **Person, seconds**: Density mean response time per person (seconds),   trimmed 99th percentile, quantile reference lines. - **Person, log**: density log-RT scale (untrimmed). - **Item, seconds**: Per-item density response times (seconds), trimmed   97.5th percentile per item, median marked. - **Item, log**: Per-item density log-RT scale (untrimmed, fixed   y-axis across items). - **Item, `overlay = TRUE`**: item densities drawn lines   single panel instead facet grid. Data generated via [sim.jhm.data()] `scale = FALSE`. Response times log scale generative model; seconds scale obtained via exponentiation.

title: Plot Simulated Response Times — plot_RT
code: plot_RT(   design = NULL,   level = "item",   logRT = FALSE,   overlay = FALSE,   N = 1000,   K = 30,   mu.person = c(0, 0),   mu.item = c(1, 0, 0.5, 1),   meanlog.sigma2 = log(0.6),   cov.m.person = matrix(c(1, 0.4, 0.4, 1), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE),   cov.m.item = matrix(c(1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0.4, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0.4, 0, 1), ncol = 4,     byrow = TRUE),   sd.item = c(0.2, 1, 0.2, 0.5),   sdlog.sigma2 = 0,   item.pars.m = NULL,   cor2cov.item = FALSE )

title: Plot Simulated Response Times — plot_RT
text: design Optional list `"sspLNIRT.design"` object holding data-generating design. supplied, fields used design-related argument caller pass explicitly (`K`, `mu.person`, `mu.item`, `meanlog.sigma2`, `cov.m.person`, `cov.m.item`, `sd.item`, `sdlog.sigma2`, `item.pars.m`, `cor2cov.item`). Caller- supplied arguments always take precedence. Extra fields `design` (e.g. `thresh`, `.par`, `seed`) ignored, object returned `$design` [get_sspLNIRT()] [optim_sample()] can passed directly. Default `NULL` (use function defaults / caller arguments). level Character. `"person"` person-level aggregates `"item"` item-level curves / distributions. logRT Logical. `TRUE`, response times shown log scale; `FALSE`, seconds scale. overlay Logical. `TRUE` `level = "item"`, item densities drawn one panel rather faceted. Ignored `level = "person"`. Default `FALSE`. item legend suppressed 12 items. N Integer. Sample size (number persons) simulated data. Default 1000. K Integer. Test length (number items). Default 10. mu.person Numeric vector length 2. Population means \((\theta, \zeta)\). mu.item Numeric vector length 4. Population means \((\alpha, \beta, \varphi, \lambda)\). meanlog.sigma2 Numeric. Mean log-normal distribution \(\sigma^2\) (log scale). cov.m.person 2x2 symmetric matrix. Covariance matrix \((\theta, \zeta)\). cov.m.item 4x4 symmetric matrix. Covariance (correlation) matrix \((\alpha, \beta, \varphi, \lambda)\). See `cor2cov.item`. sd.item Numeric vector length 4. Standard deviations \((\alpha, \beta, \varphi, \lambda)\). Required `cor2cov.item = TRUE`. sdlog.sigma2 Numeric. Standard deviation log-normal distribution \(\sigma^2\). Default 0. item.pars.m Matrix 4 columns `NULL`. supplied, item parameters held fixed instead drawn truncated MVN. cor2cov.item Logical. `TRUE`, `cov.m.item` treated correlation matrix converted using `sd.item`.

title: Plot Simulated Response Times — plot_RT
text: [ggplot2::ggplot] object.

title: Plot Simulated Response Times — plot_RT
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ plot_RT(level = "person", logRT = TRUE,  N = 500, K = 10) plot_RT(level = "item",   logRT = TRUE, N = 1000, K = 5,         mu.item = c(1, 0, 0.4, 1), sd.item = c(0.2, 1, 0.2, 0.5))  # Many items: overlay instead of faceting plot_RT(level = "item", logRT = TRUE, overlay = TRUE, N = 1000, K = 30)  # Pass a design object retrieved from get_sspLNIRT() (or optim_sample()): res <- get_sspLNIRT(thresh = 0.10, out.par = "alpha",                     K = 30, mu.alpha = 1,                     meanlog.sigma2 = log(0.6), rho = 0.4) plot_RT(res$design, level = "item", logRT = FALSE) } # }

what: Plot Estimation Metrics by Parameter Value — plot_estimation
title: Plot Estimation Metrics by Parameter Value — plot_estimation
text: Plots root mean squared error (RMSE) bias estimated parameters function true (simulated) values, aggregated bins. reveals estimation accuracy varies across parameter range (e.g., whether extreme item difficulties estimated less precisely). Accepts output [optim_sample()], [get_sspLNIRT()], [comp_rmse()]. [optim_sample()] output, error data minimum \(N\) used. data already binned (.e., `keep.err.dat = FALSE` [comp_rmse()]), bins plotted -; otherwise, raw errors binned fly using `n.bins`. item parameters, \(\sigma^2\) excluded. function preserved backward compatibility. New code prefer `plot(object, type = "estimation", ...)`.

title: Plot Estimation Metrics by Parameter Value — plot_estimation
code: plot_estimation(object, pars = "item", y.val = "rmse", n.bins = 30)

title: Plot Estimation Metrics by Parameter Value — plot_estimation
text: object object class `"sspLNIRT"`, returned [optim_sample()], [get_sspLNIRT()], [comp_rmse()]. pars Character. `"item"` `"person"`. parameter set plot. y.val Character. `"rmse"` `"bias"`. Metric y-axis. n.bins Integer. Number quantile bins aggregation. used error data full (unbinned) format. Default 30.

title: Plot Estimation Metrics by Parameter Value — plot_estimation
text: [ggplot2::ggplot] object, faceted parameter.

title: Plot Estimation Metrics by Parameter Value — plot_estimation
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ plot_estimation(result, pars = "item", y.val = "rmse") # equivalent and preferred: plot(result, type = "estimation", pars = "item", y.val = "rmse") } # }

what: Plot Power Curve from Sample Size Optimization — plot_power_curve
title: Plot Power Curve from Sample Size Optimization — plot_power_curve
text: Extracts optimization trace [optim_sample()] result, fits log-log regression (\(\log(\mathrm{RMSE}) \sim \log(N)\)), displays relationship log-log original scales side side. RMSE threshold minimum \(N\) overlaid reference lines. function preserved backward compatibility. New code prefer `plot(object, type = "power_curve", ...)`.

title: Plot Power Curve from Sample Size Optimization — plot_power_curve
code: plot_power_curve(object, out.par = NULL, thresh)

title: Plot Power Curve from Sample Size Optimization — plot_power_curve
text: object object class `"sspLNIRT"` containing `$trace` element, returned [optim_sample()] retrieved via [get_sspLNIRT()]. .par Character `NULL`. item parameter's RMSE trace plot (one `"alpha"`, `"beta"`, `"phi"`, `"lambda"`). `NULL` (default), first name `object$res.best` used; also unavailable, error raised. thresh Numeric. RMSE threshold used optimization. Must single positive number.

title: Plot Power Curve from Sample Size Optimization — plot_power_curve
text: [ggplot2::ggplot] object two facets (log-log scale   original scale).

title: Plot Power Curve from Sample Size Optimization — plot_power_curve
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ result <- get_sspLNIRT(   thresh = 0.10, out.par = "alpha",   K = 30, mu.alpha = 1,   meanlog.sigma2 = log(0.6), rho = 0.2 ) plot_power_curve(result$object, out.par = "alpha", thresh = 0.10) # equivalent and preferred: plot(result$object, type = "power_curve", out.par = "alpha", thresh = 0.10) } # }

what: Print Method for sspLNIRT Summary Objects — print.summary.sspLNIRT
title: Print Method for sspLNIRT Summary Objects — print.summary.sspLNIRT
text: Formats prints `"summary.sspLNIRT"` object console. output adapts source: [optim_sample()] results show minimum sample size, optimization diagnostics, parameter accuracy tables; [comp_rmse()] results show item person parameter accuracy tables.

title: Print Method for sspLNIRT Summary Objects — print.summary.sspLNIRT
code: # S3 method for class 'summary.sspLNIRT' print(x, digits = 4, ...)

title: Print Method for sspLNIRT Summary Objects — print.summary.sspLNIRT
text: Invisibly returns `x`.

text: Run Shiny Application

title: Run the Shiny Application — run_app
text: onStart function called app actually run. needed shinyAppObj, since shinyAppDir case, global.R file can used purpose. options Named options passed runApp call (can following: "port", "launch.browser", "host", "quiet", "display.mode" "test.mode"). can also specify width height parameters provide hint embedding environment ideal height/width app. enableBookmarking Can one "url", "server", "disable". default value, NULL, respect setting previous calls  enableBookmarking(). See enableBookmarking() information bookmarking app. uiPattern regular expression applied GET request determine whether ui used handle request. Note entire request path must match regular expression order match considered successful. ... arguments pass golem_opts. See `?golem::get_golem_options` details.

what: sspLNIRT: SampleSize Planning for the Joint Hierarchical Model — sspLNIRT-package
title: sspLNIRT: SampleSize Planning for the Joint Hierarchical Model — sspLNIRT-package
text: Sample size planning tools Joint Hierarchical Model using 2-PL normal ogive model response accuracy 3-PL log-normal model response time. Precomputed results can accessed instantly, custom designs can computed, diagnostics can used interpret results.

title: sspLNIRT: SampleSize Planning for the Joint Hierarchical Model — sspLNIRT-package
text: Maintainer: Anonymous Anonymous anonymous@example.com

what: Precomputed Sample Size Planning Results — sspLNIRT.data
title: Precomputed Sample Size Planning Results — sspLNIRT.data
text: data frame precomputed [optim_sample()] results across factorial grid design conditions Joint Hierarchical Model. row contains one parameter configuration corresponding optimization result. Use [available_configs()] view grid available conditions, [get_sspLNIRT()] retrieve results parameter values.

title: Precomputed Sample Size Planning Results — sspLNIRT.data
code: sspLNIRT.data

title: Precomputed Sample Size Planning Results — sspLNIRT.data
text: data frame two list-columns (order): cfg List `"sspLNIRT.design"` objects, containing     full set input parameters used optimization (e.g., `thresh`,     `.par`, `K`, `mu.item`, `cov.m.person`, `meanlog.sigma2`, `seed`,     etc.). res List `"sspLNIRT"` results, returned     [optim_sample()]. Contains `N.min`, `res.best`, `comp.rmse`,     `trace`. design grid varies following parameters: - `.par`: `"alpha"`, `"beta"`, `"phi"`, `"lambda"` - `thresh`: RMSE thresholds (e.g., 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20) - `K`: test length (e.g., 30, 50) - `mu.alpha`: mean discrimination (e.g., 0.6, 0.8, 1.0, 1.2, 1.4) - `meanlog.sigma2`: residual variance level (e.g., log(0.2), log(0.4),   log(0.6), log(0.8), log(1.0)) - `rho`: person parameter correlation (e.g., 0.2, 0.4, 0.6)

title: Precomputed Sample Size Planning Results — sspLNIRT.data
text: Generated [optim_sample()] `iter = 200`, `XG = 5000`,   `range = c(50, 2000)`.

title: Summarise an sspLNIRT Object — summary.sspLNIRT
text: Provides structured summary `sspLNIRT` object. method auto-detects whether object produced [optim_sample()] (contains `N.min`) [comp_rmse()] (contains `person` `item`), returns relevant fields.

title: Summarise an sspLNIRT Object — summary.sspLNIRT
code: # S3 method for class 'sspLNIRT' summary(object, ...)

title: Summarise an sspLNIRT Object — summary.sspLNIRT
text: object object class `"sspLNIRT"`, returned [optim_sample()] [comp_rmse()]. ... Additional arguments (currently unused).

title: Summarise an sspLNIRT Object — summary.sspLNIRT
text: object class `"summary.sspLNIRT"`. [optim_sample()] output, summary contains: `N.min` Integer character. Minimum sample size (boundary       message). `.par` Character vector. Target parameter(s) tracked       optimization, recovered `track.res` columns. `res.best` Named numeric vector. RMSE optimal \(N\). `comp.rmse` List. Full [comp_rmse()] output optimal       \(N\). `trace` List. Optimization diagnostics (steps, tracked results       \(N\) values, wall-clock time). [comp_rmse()] output, summary contains: `person` List `rmse`, `mc.sd.rmse`, `bias`       \(\theta\) \(\zeta\). `item` List `rmse`, `mc.sd.rmse`, `bias` item       parameters. `rhat.dat` Matrix \(\hat{R}\) values `NULL`.

title: sspLNIRT Plot Theme — theme_sspLNIRT
text: consistent ggplot2 theme used across plotting functions package. Extends [ggplot2::theme_minimal()] tighter panel grids, consistent strip styling facets, slightly larger axis text. Use final layer ggplot, e.g. `p + theme_sspLNIRT()`.

title: sspLNIRT Plot Theme — theme_sspLNIRT
code: theme_sspLNIRT(base_size = 11, base_family = "")

title: sspLNIRT Plot Theme — theme_sspLNIRT
text: base_size Numeric. Base font size points. Default `11`. base_family Character. Base font family. Default `""` (system).
